title: Geometry
objective: Students will explore geometry concepts including 2-D shapes (age 6+), Angles in triangles (age 6+), Position, direction, and movement and more.

steps.content: Let's explore 2-D shapes (age 6+)! Have you ever wondered about this?
Identify and describe properties of 2-D shapes including the number of sides and line symmetry in a vertical line
Let's practice together! Count the number of sides of a given 2-D shape
Can you count the sides of a shape and say whether it has a line of symmetry — for example, knowing that a rectangle is symmetrical but a scalene triangle is not?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about 2-D shapes (age 6+). Count the number of sides of a given 2-D shape Identify whether a 2-D shape has a vertical line of symmetry
Let's discover the amazing world of Angles in triangles (age 6+)!
Distinguish defining attributes of shapes (e.g. triangles are closed and three-sided) from non-defining attributes (e.g. colour, orientation, overall size)
Let's practice together! Build and draw shapes that possess defining attributes
Does you understand that what makes a shape a triangle is having 3 sides and 3 corners — not its colour or which way it's pointing?

Great job! Today we learned about Angles in triangles (age 6+). Build and draw shapes that possess defining attributes Identify that a shape remains a triangle regardless of its size, colour, or orientation
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Position, direction, and movement!

Use mathematical vocabulary to describe position, direction, and movement, including straight lines and distinguishing rotation as a turn in terms of right angles (quarter, half, three-quarter turns, clockwise and anti-clockwise)
Time to try this out: Use terms such as clockwise, anti-clockwise, quarter turn, half turn, three-quarter turn
Can you follow directions like "turn clockwise a quarter turn" or "move three squares to the right" on a grid or in a simple navigation game?
Great job! Today we learned about Position, direction, and movement. Use terms such as clockwise, anti-clockwise, quarter turn, half turn, three-quarter turn Describe a right angle as a quarter turn
Let's explore Edges, vertices, and faces! Have you ever wondered about this?
Identify and describe properties of 3-D shapes including the number of edges, vertices, and faces
Let me show you something cool. Count the edges, vertices, and faces of common 3-D shapes
Can you count the faces, edges, and vertices of a 3-D shape like a cube or triangular prism?
Great job! Today we learned about Edges, vertices, and faces. Count the edges, vertices, and faces of common 3-D shapes Use the terms edge, vertex (vertices), and face correctly
Today we're going to learn about Sorting 2-D and 3-D shapes. Get ready for an adventure!
Compare and sort common 2-D and 3-D shapes and everyday objects by their properties
Time to try this out: Sort a collection of 2-D shapes by number of sides
Can you sort a collection of shapes into groups — for example, separating all the shapes with curved sides from those with only straight sides?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Sorting 2-D and 3-D shapes: Sort a collection of 2-D shapes by number of sides Sort 3-D shapes by number of faces or whether faces are flat/curved
Let's explore Building with 3-D Shapes! Have you ever wondered about this?
Compose three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, right circular cylinders) and create composite shapes; build new shapes from component shapes
Time to try this out: Stack cubes and prisms to build towers or structures
Can you combine 3-D shapes — like stacking cubes and cylinders — to build a new composite solid?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Building with 3-D Shapes: Stack cubes and prisms to build towers or structures Combine 3-D shapes to make a new solid (e.g. cone on top of cylinder)
Let's explore Composing Shapes! Have you ever wondered about this?
Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, quarter-circles) to create composite shapes, and compose new shapes from the composite shape
Time to try this out: Combine two triangles to form a rectangle or larger triangle
Can you use flat shapes like triangles and rectangles to fit together and make a new larger shape — like a puzzle?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Composing Shapes. Combine two triangles to form a rectangle or larger triangle Put together half-circles and quarter-circles to form circles or new shapes
Let's explore 2-D faces on 3-D shapes! Have you ever wondered about this?
Identify 2-D shapes on the surface of 3-D shapes (e.g. a circle on a cylinder, a triangle on a pyramid)
Time to try this out: Point out a circular face on a cylinder or cone
Can you look at a 3-D shape and name the 2-D shapes on its faces — for example, saying a cylinder has circles on the ends and a rectangle wrapped round the side?
You did it! Here's what we explored about 2-D faces on 3-D shapes: Point out a circular face on a cylinder or cone Identify triangular faces on a pyramid
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Patterns & Sequences!
Order and arrange combinations of mathematical objects in patterns and sequences
Let me show you something cool. Continue a repeating pattern of shapes or colours
Can you spot a repeating pattern made of shapes or objects and continue it, or create their own repeating pattern?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Patterns & Sequences. Continue a repeating pattern of shapes or colours Describe the rule for a given sequence of objects
